title: Checkout API 504s during evening peak
symptoms: p99 latency climbed from 180ms to 30s over 20 minutes. Gateway returned 504s on ~40% of POST /checkout. CPU flat, memory flat. Database CPU normal.
root_cause: Connection pool exhaustion. A retry added two weeks earlier did not release its connection on the timeout path, so every timed-out request permanently leaked one pooled connection.
resolution: Rolled back the retry change, then reissued it with the connection acquired inside a context manager. Raised pool max from 20 to 50 as a buffer and added a pool-utilization alert at 80%.
lesson: Flat CPU with climbing latency almost always means the service is waiting on a finite resource, not computing. Check pool utilization before you check code hot paths.

title: Search results stale by up to 6 hours
symptoms: Users reported newly created listings missing from search. Indexer showed no errors and its dashboard was green. Kafka consumer lag was near zero.
root_cause: The indexer was silently dropping messages whose payload exceeded a 1MB limit. The drop path logged at DEBUG, which was filtered out in production, so the dashboard stayed green.
resolution: Raised the payload limit, moved the drop path to ERROR level, and added a dropped-message counter to the dashboard.
lesson: A green dashboard only proves the metrics you chose are healthy. When behavior and metrics disagree, suspect the metrics.

title: Payment webhooks processed twice
symptoms: Small number of customers double-charged. Webhook handler logs showed the same event id processed by two workers within 200ms of each other.
root_cause: Idempotency check used a read-then-write against a cache with no locking. Two workers both read 'not seen' before either wrote.
resolution: Replaced the cache check with a unique constraint on event_id in the primary database, letting the write itself enforce idempotency.
lesson: Idempotency belongs in a uniqueness constraint at the storage layer, never in an application-level read-then-write.

title: Nightly ETL job silently truncating rows
symptoms: Weekly revenue report came in 12% low. ETL job reported success every night. Row counts in the destination were consistently lower than source.
root_cause: A batch insert used ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING against a table whose unique key had been narrowed in a migration, so legitimately distinct rows collided and were discarded.
resolution: Restored the composite unique key and backfilled 9 days of data. Added a source-vs-destination row count assertion to the job.
lesson: ON CONFLICT DO NOTHING converts data loss into a successful exit code. Any job that uses it needs an independent row count check.

title: Auth service latency spike after region failover
symptoms: After a planned us-east-1 failover, login latency in eu-west-1 went from 90ms to 1.4s. Error rate stayed at zero. Traffic volume unchanged.
root_cause: Session lookups were still being routed to the primary region because the read replica config was pinned to a hardcoded endpoint from an old deploy.
resolution: Moved endpoint resolution to service discovery and added a cross-region latency SLO alert.
lesson: Zero errors plus multiplied latency after a failover means traffic is crossing a region boundary it shouldn't. Look at endpoint config before database performance.

title: Image uploads failing for large files only
symptoms: Uploads over roughly 8MB returned 502. Smaller uploads fine. No entries in the application log at all for failed requests.
root_cause: Load balancer request body limit was lower than the application's configured limit, so the LB rejected the request before it ever reached the app.
resolution: Aligned the LB body limit with the application limit and added a synthetic large-file upload check.
lesson: If a failing request produces no application log line, the failure happened upstream of the application. Work outward from the app, not inward.

title: Feature flag evaluation blocking request thread
symptoms: Homepage TTFB doubled after a release. CPU flat. The flag provider's status page reported degraded performance in one region.
root_cause: The SDK was initialized in blocking mode with no timeout, so every page render waited on a remote flag fetch.
resolution: Switched to the SDK's local cache mode with a background refresh and a 50ms hard timeout defaulting to the last known value.
lesson: Any third-party call in a request path needs a timeout and a fallback value, and flag SDKs are the ones people forget.

title: Cron duplicated after Kubernetes node drain
symptoms: Monthly invoices generated twice for a subset of accounts. Job history showed two runs 4 minutes apart on the same schedule.
root_cause: A node drain caused the CronJob controller to reschedule a job it could not confirm had completed, and the job had no run-level locking.
resolution: Added a database-backed advisory lock keyed on the billing period, so a second run exits immediately.
lesson: Schedulers guarantee at-least-once, not exactly-once. Any job with side effects needs its own lock keyed on the logical work unit.
